title: Correctness, Performance, Visibility. Pick Three.
summary: How a Rust port became a deeper exercise in designing the boundaries between a matching engine, an exchange, and a real-time UI.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Back in 2022, I got my first exposure to professional software engineering through finance, and it completely changed how I viewed the industry.

At [BAM](https://www.bamfunds.com/), I saw analysts, developers, and engineers all using code to solve real problems. That challenged the picture I had in my head of finance as mostly spreadsheets and reports. Instead, I found an environment where software sat close to research, trading, risk, and decision-making.

What stayed with me most was learning about quantitative developers: engineers building systems that operate close to the execution of trades. I was drawn to the idea that markets are not just abstract financial systems, but real technical systems made of orderbooks, matching engines, protocols, latency constraints, and infrastructure decisions. That curiosity is what eventually led me to build my own matching engine in Rust.

sections.heading: Why Rust?
sections.body:
It is hard to pin the choice down to one reason, but the main motivation was that I wanted to become a more systems-minded developer. C++ still dominates many low-latency trading systems, and that is not changing anytime soon, but Rust gave me a way to explore similar problems while being forced to think carefully about ownership, mutation, and memory layout.

I did not build this project because Rust magically makes matching engines easy. In some places, it made the implementation more awkward. But that awkwardness was useful. The borrow checker forced me to be explicit about when I was reading from the book, when I was mutating it, and which part of the system actually owned each piece of state.

sections.heading: What's an Orderbook
sections.body: Here's the crux of the write up. At a high level, an orderbook is a collection of all buy and sell orders for some security, sorted by price.

Bids represent people willing to buy, Asks represent people willing to sell. Sorting by price is relevant because bids and asks get matched when the best (highest) bid is >= the best (lowest) ask.

Its also important to note that most orderbooks operate under the idea of price-time priority. Macthes occur first at the best prices, then at the earliest arrivals.

sections.heading: Core Data Structures
sections.body:
Bids and asks are represented using `BTreeMap<Price, Vec<OrderId>>`where each price level maps to a queue of order IDs resting at that price.

This choice fits the shape of an orderbook. Prices are not just values to look up; they form a sorted ladder. The engine constantly needs to ask questions like: what is the highest bid, what is the lowest ask, and which price levels are executable for this incoming order?

A regular `HashMap` would make exact price lookups easy, but it would not preserve order. To find the best bid or ask, the engine would have to scan through all price levels. A BTreeMap keeps prices sorted, so the book can efficiently access the lowest ask and highest bid without searching through every level.

There is also an interesting memory-layout reason this structure is appealing. A traditional binary search tree stores one key per node, which can lead to lots of heap allocations and pointer chasing. Rust’s BTreeMap stores multiple keys per node, which better matches how modern CPUs like to read memory: in contiguous chunks. In practice, that means a B-tree can trade a few extra comparisons inside a node for fewer cache misses and better locality.

sections.caption: rough idea, more nuance in rust docs

sections.body:
And so the idea is that the bids and the asks hold keys to sorted price levels that map to a queues. This lets best prices and by extension best bids/asks get accessed in constant time on average. 

The actual orders are represented with `HashMap<OrderId, Order>`. This is the source of truth for order information such as price, quantity and side (buy/sell)

We also have an order index, `HashMap<OrderId, OrderEntry>`, which allows us to access orders in the bid/ask maps in constant time for modification and deletion. 

There's a tradeoff to be noted here; in some implementations, the order queue is a doubly linked list which aids in retaining the queue order after deletion. However for the sake of simplicity, and optimization benefits described before, we opt for a dynamic array, even though we end up with worse case O(n) deletions because of left shifting of the array.

Fianlly we have another map, `HashMap<Price, LevelData>` which gives aggregated information about a price level. This tells you the total quantity and orders at a current level, which is useful for specific order types I'll talk about later.

sections.heading: Skill-Based Matchmaking
sections.body:
The matching engine is less mysterious than it sounds. At its core, it repeatedly compares the best bid and best ask. If the highest buyer is willing to pay at least as much as the lowest seller is asking, a trade can happen.

Each iteration does a small number of things:
We'll take a look at the code as well because the borrow checker informs the order that operations need to happen in the matching loop.

Here's we initalize resulting `trades` and `filled_orders`, which we'll aggregate later to created our `MatchResult`. We start a loop, and we do our common sense checks and get our necessary info.

A big part of Rust's ownership model is it's rules around mutable and immutable references. At any given time you can have any number of immutable references or one mutable reference but not both. Take a look at the following:

Reads and writes to the orders map happen seperately within thier own scope to ensure both types of references to the don't exist at the same time.

After this, we check to see if either order was filled, remove them from the book if they are, and return the results.

sections.heading: Flavors of Limit Order
sections.body:
While on the topic of matching and orders heres some common order types I decided to cover.

`GoodTilCancel`: Fill what you can, rest the remainder

`FillAndKill`: Fill what you can, cancel the remainder

`FillOrKill`:  Only execute if the full quantity is avaliable

`Market`: Execute immediately against avaliable liquidity

FillOrKill exposed a useful optimization boundary. To decide whether an order can fully execute, the engine does not need to inspect every individual resting order. It only needs to know whether there is enough aggregate liquidity across executable price levels.

Imagine 1,000 orders sitting at the same price level. A naive can_fully_fill implementation could walk every order at that level and sum the remaining quantity one by one. But if the book already maintains aggregate level data, the engine can treat that entire level as one number.

That made per-level data more than a UI convenience. It became part of the matching engine’s validation path.
